mnemonics.title: MongoDB CRUD Operations
mnemonics.content: CRUD in MongoDB: Create - insertOne()/insertMany(), Read - find()/findOne(), Update - updateOne()/updateMany() with $set, Delete - deleteOne()/deleteMany(). Remember: 'I FRY U Deli' - Insert Find Replace Update You Delete

mnemonics.title: Aggregation Pipeline Stages
mnemonics.content: Pipeline stages in order: $match (filter) → $project (select) → $sort (order) → $group (aggregate) → $limit/$skip (paginate). Remember: 'MaP SiG LoS' - Match Project Sort Group Limit Skip

mnemonics.title: NoSQL Types Memory Trick
mnemonics.content: NoSQL types: Document (MongoDB), Key-Value (Redis), Column-family (Cassandra), Graph (Neo4j). Remember: 'DoKCG' - Document Oriented Key Column Graph

acronyms.usage: Database systems that provide flexible schemas and horizontal scalability
Binary-encoded format MongoDB uses to store documents
Theorem describing trade-offs in distributed databases
Transaction properties (MongoDB supports multi-document ACID since v4.0)
Tools like Mongoose that map objects to MongoDB documents

visualTips.visualization: Think of a MongoDB document like a JavaScript object with key-value pairs, nested objects like addresses within customer documents, and arrays for multi-valued fields like phone numbers or tags

Table = rows (fixed columns) → Collection = documents (flexible fields). Visualize a collection as a folder holding various shaped papers (documents) while table is like a spreadsheet with fixed columns
Imagine dividing a large book into chapters and storing each chapter on different shelves. Each shelf is a shard, and MongoDB knows which shelf to look for based on the shard key
Think of replica set as a master copy (primary) and multiple photocopies (secondaries). If master is damaged, one photocopy becomes the new master automatically

analogies.analogy: Collections are like folders in a file cabinet - you can put different types of documents in each, unlike rigid file cabinets with labeled tabs (tables)
MongoDB schema is like a polymorphic form - one person might fill all fields, another might leave some blank, and a third might add new fields not on the original form
Indexes in MongoDB are like the index at the back of a textbook - finding a topic is instant instead of flipping through every page
Think of aggregation like a factory assembly line: raw documents enter, each stage transforms/proceses them, and finished results come out the other end
